model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget, the most leverage comes from processing more examples per step. Increasing batch size should cover much more of CIFAR-10 within the same step budget, and pairing that with AdamW plus a modest learning-rate and weight-decay increase is a simple way to keep optimization stable and improve early-budget convergence.","secondary_modes":["indexing","topk","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimizer and schedule now use the fixed-step budget more effectively, so the next simple lever is modestly increasing hidden width. This raises MLP capacity without changing the training loop or adding fragile complexity, and it should help the model make better use of the larger effective data coverage from the 256 batch.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last width increase improved validation loss again, which suggests the current optimizer and schedule can still use more MLP capacity within the fixed budget. A further modest width bump is the simplest continuation of that trend, with lower risk than changing depth or the training loop.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Width has produced three consecutive improvements, so the most evidence-backed next move is another modest increase rather than switching to a new mechanism prematurely. This keeps the script simple and continues scaling the same part of the model that is already paying off under the fixed-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Capacity scaling is still helping, but the marginal gain is shrinking. Adding light label smoothing is a simple regularization change that often improves validation loss for image classification without disrupting the current optimizer, schedule, or model structure.","secondary_modes":["layout","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"Label smoothing made validation loss worse on this workload, so the highest-confidence next move is to remove it and restore the stronger baseline configuration. That keeps the script simple and avoids compounding a regressive regularization change while we continue searching from the best-performing regime.","secondary_modes":["micro","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Under a fixed 256-step budget, batch size is still one of the highest-leverage knobs because it changes how much of the dataset the model can absorb before evaluation. Moving from 256 to 384 keeps the earlier batching strategy but pushes data coverage further, and a small learning-rate increase compensates for the larger batch without otherwise changing the training setup.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.4e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 384","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last change delivered the largest gain so far, which strongly suggests the fixed-step budget still favors higher per-step data coverage.
